title: Limoncello
excerpt: Limoncello. A home-cook's recipe from Rebecca's collection, kept in the family rotation.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook, a recipe she's made many times in her own kitchen.

content.text: Limoncello. A British home-cook's dish, the kind of recipe families adjust across generations. Adjustable for what you need.
A weeknight-friendly recipe: most of the work is the prep, and the cooking takes care of itself.

1. Put the sugar and water in a saucepan over medium heat. Stir to dissolve the sugar. Bring to the boil, then simmer briskly for 3-4 minutes until the bubbles look syrupy. Leave to cool. 2. Add the lemon zest and juice to the syrup while it is still slightly warm. Then add the eau-de-vie or vodka. 3. Pour into sterile bottles or jars and seal. 4. Leave in a cool, dark place for 1 month, shaking every day for the first week. After a month the lemon liqueur will be ready to drink. Strain into clean bottles. 5. Put in the freezer for several hours before serving. The liqueur is best served in frozen shot glasses.

attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.fix: Salt a pinch at a time, tasting. A squeeze of lemon or splash of vinegar at the end lifts most savoury dishes.
Pull off the heat a minute or two before you think it's done. Plate straight away, or transfer to a warmed serving dish.
items.cause: Carryover cooking, food keeps cooking on the hot pan after the heat goes off.
items.symptom: It's overcooked by the time it reaches the table

items.fix: Drop the heat and give it longer. A probe thermometer is the only reliable check for meat.
items.cause: Heat too high, browned the outside before the middle warmed through.

items.fix: Reheat covered with a splash of stock or water, on a low heat. Microwave on 60% power if using one, gentler than full blast.
items.cause: Reheated uncovered, or no liquid in the pan.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong

content.text: Vegetarian swap: replace meat with mushrooms, lentils, or beans, adjust seasoning to compensate.
Spice version: a pinch of chilli flakes or cracked black pepper at the end shifts the dish without losing its character.
Best fresh. Leftovers keep two days in the fridge; reheat covered with a splash of liquid.
A British home cook's dish. It sits among the recipes families remember without thinking about, the kind that gets adjusted across generations and never quite the same twice.
